title: Craig A. Parsons and Timothy M. Smeeding (Eds): Immigration and the Transformation of Europe.
abstract: The title of the opening chapter of this book, written by the two editors, effectively captures the big question addressed in the book: ‘What is unique about immigration in Europe?’ As a consequence of addressing this question the chapters collected in this book have a strong comparative base, comparing immigrants in various European societies, as well as including the traditional immigration countries of Australia, Canada and the USA in the comparisons (for instance the chapters of Morissens; Pedersen et al.; Schain; Schnepf; van Tubergen). The importance of including these traditional immigration countries lies in the dominance of their particular histories of immigration in the research agendas and the policy debates about migration in Europe. But these particular histories might be unique, or at least less relevant for current immigration into Europe. The intra-European comparisons, made in all chapters, are also important, because immigration is not (any longer) a problem of some nations, related to their colonial past, or their particular history. It has become a problem for all members of the European Union, even for those EU countries which have been ‘sending’ countries in the past (Greece, Ireland, Italy, Portugal, Spain) or are currently ‘sending’ countries (Poland, Czech Republic, Romania). The chapter of Andall on the Ghanaian migration shows that it is directed to Europe as a whole, and not to a specific EU country. The preference for a specific EU country is mostly related to the possibilities offered by their national laws and policies of breaching ‘Fortress Europe’. Therefore any analysis of immigration to Europe, which focuses on one EU country of destination, runs a large risk of being biased by an overemphasis on the importance of national immigration policies and an underestimation of the importance of the origin composition of their immigrant population. Thus, the first attractive aspect of this book is its consequential comparative, non-parochial approach, which is too often missing in the research on immigration due to its continuing focus on the traditional immigration countries.
